# Transcrição: 12-Bayleaf in Throat

- **Fonte:** 12-Bayleaf in Throat
- **Duração:** 07:46
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Keep it going! Thank you, sir. Thank you. It starts with a little cough. There's something in my throat. I cough again. It doesn't come up. I reach for a glass of water. I'm in a restaurant, a busy dining room, having lunch with my wife. I gulp the water. I try to force whatever's caught in my throat down and it doesn't go. So I sort of turn away from the table and I start coughing loud, trying to force it up. My wife reaches over and she squeezes my forearm. She asks if I'm okay. She knows I'm okay. We're both schoolteachers and just yesterday we had our recertification for CPR. She knows that if I'm coughing or I'm talking, I'm not really choking and everything's fine, except I'm not fine because something is caught in my throat and it's a real thing. And then my body decides it needs to get it out, so I start coughing involuntarily like that awful, hacking, phlemy, uncontrollable, terrible sound. And I do it for a long time. And when I stop, the waiter's standing next to me and he says, are you all right? And I want to say, does that sound like I'm all right? Like what sound do you think makes me sound okay? This is terrible. Like I have a fish bone, I think, in my throat because your chef left it in my fish stew. No, I'm not all right. And normally I would say all these things to him because my superpower in life is saying the worst thing at the right moment to make someone cry. I am the best at that. I have done it all my life. But I can't because there's something caught in my throat. And then I start coughing again and it is, again, that uncontrollable hacking sound and I can hear the dining room getting quiet as I cough. And when I stop, the manager is now at the table and he says, sir, would you like to go to the bar? And I'm thinking, is there a medical team in the bar that I don't know about? Is there a magical power that the bar has? I know what he wants, which is please get out of the dining room because you're disturbing everybody. But I want to say I'm here because of you and your chef. And this is bad, but I can't say any of it because I'm afraid now. There's something in my throat and it will not come out. So I get up and I just walk to the bar. And as I get into the bar, the waiter comes behind me and I know what he's going to do. He's going to heimlich me, which is wrong because first I'm coughing and talking so I don't need it. And he also, he's grabbing me here by the chest, which just means he's probably going to break ribs. And I want to tell him, no, don't do it. You're an idiot, but I'm afraid I am coughing on something and I'm thinking I'm not breathing that well. And so I let him just pick me up in the air and he's crushing my chest and I'm crying out. And finally it's my wife who says, put him down. She says, call 911. And I think, don't call 911. I have like a fish bone in my throat. What are you crazy? But I'm also thinking there's something in my throat and I am really frightened. Get someone to help me.

## [03:00 - 06:03]

And so 15 minutes later, I'm in the emergency room and there's a young doctor in front of me and he says, what's wrong? And I say, I think I have a fish bone in my throat and I see him deflate. He's like, oh, this is not that interesting. But he says, no problem. We can take care of it. He comes back with what looks like salad tongs and an aerosol spray. He says, I'm going to spray your throat and I'm going to reach down and pull out whatever is in there. It's going to be fine. He says, if you could suppress your gag reflex, that would be helpful. I'm like, do you not know the definition of a reflex? Like who is in front of me? I want to ask for another doctor, but again, I am just, I'm terrified right now. I'm really thinking I'm having a hard time breathing. There's a thing in my throat. And so I let him do it. He sprays my throat. The salad tongs go down. My gag reflex fires off. They get two nurses to hold me down. For 15 minutes we battle. And by the time he's sweating and the thing is still in there and he says, we need to try something else. So then a slightly older doctor arrives with a different device. It's a snake with a little light and a camera and clippers. And he says, we're going to put this up your nose and then down your throat and pull it right out. It's going to be fine. There's a lot of questions I want to ask about what's about to happen to me. But I'm so afraid I don't say anything. And it's not as bad as I think it's going to be. The first time it goes up, it feels like it's going to pop right out of your eye. But it takes a hard left and then goes down. And for about half an hour he's looking through a scope trying to find this thing that he can't find. And finally he pulls it out and he says, we need to try something else. And so half an hour later an old man arrives, the oldest doctor in the hospital. And he says, we're going to roll you into surgery. He says, whatever's in your throat, we're afraid you're going to cough and it's going to end up in your lung, which is going to be terrible. So we're going to cut you open from your neck down to here and we're going to get it out that way. And I'm thinking, my God, it's a fishbone, I think. But I don't say a word. I'm so afraid. And so I'm rolling to surgery, ceiling tiles going by, my wife holding my hand. I'm 50 hallway feet from someone cutting me open from stem to stern to get something out when finally I find my voice. And I say, stop. And they do. They all listen. The doctors and the nurses around me, they all stop. And I say, listen, this is crazy. You can't cut me open. Think outside the box, people, please. And it's like five seconds of silence. And then a nurse says, we've never taken the thing that we put up and down your nose down someone's throat before. We could try that. And I say, yeah, let's try that. And I see the doctor's eyes just widen. He is so excited. And so 15 minutes later, I'm in a small room with like 20 doctors and two people with video cameras. And they've pushed my wife to the back of the room and there's two nurses ready to hold me down. They're so excited. I am now secondary to what is happening because we're taking a medical device and using it in a new way.

## [06:03 - 07:46]

If this works, this is huge. And so they make sure the video cameras are running and the two nurses hold me down and the thing slides down my throat and my gag reflex fires off. And for about 15 minutes, he's using the scope down my throat while my body battles him. And everyone is just hushed and waiting to see if this will work. And then the doctor says, and he starts to pull that snake out of my throat. And when it passes by my mouth, I can see on the end of the clippers a bay leaf perfectly sized to land in my throat and sit there. And as soon as it comes out, the doctor turns to the video guys and says, do you got it? And they say, we got it. And it's high fives and I am irrelevant. I can see my wife through the high fiving doctors. We make a momentary connection and we smile for the first time all day long. And I learn that when you are frightened, you are at your most vulnerable. Truly. You stop talking and you allow people to do things to you without asking any questions. And I learned that day that when you are most frightened, that is the time that you have to find your voice. That you have to say something. That you cannot allow your fear to have you whisked down a hallway to get cut open when there are other alternatives. It is when we are most frightened that we have to speak up and make sure that we are heard. Thank you. Thank you.
